items.passage: Genes are the chemical instructions that determine how every living organism grows and functions. They are written in the sequence of DNA, a long molecule coiled tightly inside each cell. When a cell divides, this code is copied so that both new cells inherit identical information. Tiny changes called mutations sometimes alter a gene, and these variations can spread through later generations. Over vast stretches of time, such inherited differences allow whole populations to adapt to changing surroundings.

Green architecture seeks to design buildings that consume less energy and harm the environment as little as possible. Architects orient walls and windows carefully so that natural sunlight reduces the need for electric lighting. Thick insulation and shaded glass keep interiors comfortable without relying heavily on costly heating or cooling. Some structures even collect rainwater on their roofs and grow gardens that cool the surrounding air. Through these thoughtful choices, designers prove that comfort and conservation can exist together in modern cities.

Group dynamics describes the invisible forces that shape how people behave when they gather together. Within a team, members often adopt unspoken roles, and a few individuals quietly steer the wider discussion. Pressure to agree can grow so strong that people hesitate to voice doubts about a popular plan. Skilled leaders therefore encourage open debate, ensuring that quieter voices contribute before any decision becomes final. By understanding these patterns, organizations can build groups that cooperate smoothly yet still think independently.

Ancient astronomers tracked the movements of the Sun, Moon, and stars long before the telescope existed. By watching the sky night after night, they noticed regular patterns that helped them measure the passing year. Many early civilizations built great stone monuments aligned precisely with the rising sun at midsummer. These careful observations guided farmers, who depended on the calendar to plant and harvest their crops on time. Such knowledge, passed down through generations, became the foundation for later scientific discovery.

Erosion is the slow process by which wind, water, and ice gradually wear away the surface of the land. Rushing rivers carry tiny grains of rock downstream, carving deep valleys over thousands of years. Along the coast, crashing waves break apart cliffs and scatter the loosened sand across distant beaches. Freezing water expands inside cracks, splitting solid boulders into smaller fragments that tumble down steep slopes. Though each change seems small, these forces continually reshape mountains and reveal the planet's restless nature.

A planet's atmosphere is the layer of gases that surrounds it and shapes conditions on its surface. The composition of these gases varies enormously, from the thick carbon dioxide of Venus to the thin air of Mars. Gravity holds the atmosphere in place, while heat from the nearby star stirs powerful winds and storms. On some worlds, dense clouds trap warmth and raise temperatures far above what sunlight alone would produce. Studying these distant skies helps scientists understand how climates form and evolve across the universe.
